endings.title: The End of an Era
endings.reason: Your body finally gives out — the decades of excess and grievance burned through it faster than even the doctors predicted. The empire outlives the man, as empires always do. {the_nation} files its papers on {full_name} and moves on without ceremony. History is indifferent to the size of the casket.

endings.title: The Dynasty That Never Was
endings.reason: The {surname} line never takes root in America — no fortune, no Queens, no 1946 birth. The branch of history that needed this family simply closes over without a scar. There is no {given_name}, no tower, no movement, no mythology. The world proceeds without noticing what it missed.

endings.title: A Life Cut Short
endings.reason: A reckless boyhood dare ends the story before it can begin. The {surname} name carries on without you — Fred will find another heir, the rentals will change hands, the fortune will survive. Only the particular swagger of {given_name} dies here, before it ever became something the world had to reckon with.

endings.title: Washed Out
endings.reason: Expelled and disowned, you drift into a small, anonymous life far from any tower. The ambitions your father tried to stamp into you evaporate in the fluorescent light of a middle-management desk. You are not a villain, not a hero — just a man who almost became someone, in a country full of them.

endings.title: No Empire to Inherit
endings.reason: The discrimination cases stuck, Fred was indicted, and the rental fortune dissolved into fines and lawyers' fees. With no seed capital there is no Manhattan, no tower, no golden escalator. {given_name} {surname} lives out a working life — comfortable enough, broadly unknown — and the name inspires nothing stronger than mild recognition at a church potluck.

endings.title: Quiet Succession
endings.reason: You never reached for more than enough. A comfortable, unglamorous CEO of the outer-borough rentals — wealthy but not insanely so, still married to your first wife — you hand the business to your son just as your father handed it to you. The grandiosity never caught. The story ends peaceably, and perhaps that is its own kind of victory.

endings.title: Cut Down
endings.reason: A bullet finds you at the height of your notoriety. The crowd screams; the cameras keep rolling; {the_nation} fractures along every fault line you spent decades widening. You are mourned by half the country and quietly relieved-over by the other half, and neither half will ever admit to the truth of it.

endings.title: Toppled
endings.reason: Investigations close in from every angle and your inner circle abandons you one defection at a time. {head_of_state_office} is vacated by men with guns and lawyers and no patience left for your maneuvering. You leave {seat_of_power} smaller than you entered it, and the door locks behind you.

endings.title: Convicted
endings.reason: The cases finally stick — all the paperwork, all the phone calls, all the favors that seemed so untouchable in the light of power look different under oath. The cell door closes on the most famous man alive. Outside, {the_nation} debates what it all means. Inside, there is only the particular silence of a man who mistook loudness for invincibility.

endings.title: Ruined
endings.reason: The debt avalanche buries you with the methodical patience of mathematics. The name comes off the buildings — first the Atlantic City towers, then the New York ones, then the licensed-product spinoffs — until the brand that sold a lifestyle of winning is auctioned off in receivership. The lesson was always there in the numbers. You just never read them.

endings.title: A Quiet Life
endings.reason: You step off the stage early and find, to your mild surprise, that the air is cleaner out here. History barely records your name — a footnote in a footnote, {given_name} {surname}, who might have been someone. The crowds you might have commanded never assemble. The damage you might have done never lands. The quiet is its own verdict.

endings.title: The People's Patriarch
endings.reason: You chose solidarity over self — or at least, you chose it loudly enough and long enough that the choice became real. A post-scarcity commonwealth rises in your name, and the name {surname} loses its gilded connotation and acquires something stranger: sincerity. History, which had every reason to expect worse from you, finds itself pleasantly surprised.

endings.title: God-Emperor
endings.reason: Unbound by law or shame or the quiet check of anyone who loved you for the right reasons, you rule {the_nation} as a living god. The world kneels — and seethes in a register you have trained yourself not to hear. {head_of_state} {surname} will be remembered. The historians who survive will make sure of it.

endings.title: The Tide of History
endings.reason: You did not bend the age to your will — you rode it with better timing than anyone expected. The world had already turned toward the commons, and {given_name} {surname} became the face it chose: unlikely, improbable, and somehow apt. A post-scarcity commonwealth, inevitable in hindsight, carries your name into centuries that will argue about whether you deserved it.

endings.title: Sovereign of a Willing World
endings.reason: The age itself had soured toward strongmen, and you were the strongest one available. No coup was needed — {the_nation} and then the wider world had already decided they wanted a master, and you merely accepted the appointment with the practiced ease of a man who always knew it was coming. A living god atop a planet that asked to kneel, and will spend the next thousand years regretting the asking.

endings.title: Two-World Patriarch
endings.reason: {head_of_state} {surname} — immortal, insufferable, unkillable — stands as master of Earth and father of the first Martian city. A civilization spanning two worlds needed exactly the kind of man who would never stop claiming credit for it. History, for once, agrees he has a point.

endings.title: Heaven on Earth
endings.reason: You never reached for the stars — but you remade the one world you had. A serene, almost religious golden age settles over {the_nation} and then, quietly, over most of the planet. {full_name} chose the bounded sky, and under it built something that looks, from the right angle, like enough. Future generations will debate whether it was wisdom or failure of imagination. The living don't much care.

endings.title: The Deity of a Closed World
endings.reason: No Mars, no stars — only Earth, and you above it. {full_name} sealed the sky and called it heaven, and the generation that grew up under his reign knows no other word for it. A deistic god-king worshipped on a planet that never looked up, because looking up was quietly made illegal.

endings.title: An Earthbound Twilight
endings.reason: No Mars, no stars, no crown, no ruin — just a long life on one small world that ran its course and ended when it was supposed to. History files {full_name} under the ordinarily powerful: a name that mattered for a season, shaped a few decades, and then became a footnote that academics fight over at conferences no one else attends. The sky stayed closed, and you never much minded.

endings.title: The Gift of the Stars
endings.reason: Because humanity reached out from Mars — because {full_name} pushed the program far enough and long enough for the signal to cross the right distances — the Concord saw us, assessed us, and judged us worthy of the gift. They gave humanity faster-than-light travel the way a civilization gives a child a book: freely, with the quiet hope that it will be used well. Whether it is, is the next story.

endings.title: What Answered
endings.reason: The extra-solar wake of our expansion read, to something old and very far away, as prey. It tracked the signal home with the patient efficiency of a predator that has done this before. {full_name} built the beacon. Humanity lit it. What answered was not interested in the distinction.

endings.title: Among the Stars, As Equals
endings.reason: Allied with the Concord and warp-borne, humanity spread across the light-years as a respected peer of worlds — not a subject species, not a conquered curiosity, but a civilization that earned its seat at a table older than its sun. {full_name} began the chain of choices that ended here. The Concord archives will record the name, correctly spelled, for as long as stars burn.

endings.title: Emperor of a Thousand Suns
endings.reason: You conquered what came, turned the gift of the stars into a slaving empire, and built a dominion of cruelty at interstellar scale. Immortal {head_of_state} of a darkened galaxy, {full_name} achieved exactly what he always said he would — and the universe, which has seen this before, is already counting the centuries until the resistance wins.

endings.title: The Long Rebellion
endings.reason: Subjugated, then unbroken — humanity threw off its overlords across a century of insurgency that no single generation lived to see completed. The grandchildren of the conquered became the liberators. Free at last, scarred deeply, and finally sovereign over its own fate, {the_nation} and the species beyond it emerge into a galaxy that respects what was paid for this.

endings.title: Administrator of a Conquered World
endings.reason: You kept {head_of_state_office} by handing humanity its chains — a figurehead of alien masters ruling an occupied Earth, your face on the propaganda and your signature on the compliance orders. {full_name}: the name that will mean, for as long as the species survives to remember it, the precise shape of choosing self over kind.

endings.title: Silence
endings.reason: The rebellion failed. The signal we sent read as invitation; the thing that answered treated it as one. What came across the dark finished what it started with the thoroughness of a species that does not leave survivors to complicate the record. The human story ends here, in the dark, with no one left to write the epitaph.

endings.title: The New Order Perfected
endings.reason: The Reich stabilized into something almost serene by its own lights — the eugenics boards filled with bureaucrats who preferred order to cruelty, the conquest zones quietly integrated, the flags unchanged but the firing squads retired. {given_name} {surname} governed the hemisphere's economy in a world where that word, utopia, was genuinely spoken without irony by its architects. What it took to build it and who it excluded were questions the citizens had learned not to raise. You provided what the system asked for. The system called it flourishing.

endings.title: The Administered Reich
endings.reason: Neither the dream nor the nightmare fully materialized — just governance, Reich-style, competently maintained. The ideology ossified into bureaucratic habit, the racial laws became paperwork, and the hemisphere's commerce ran on efficient rails toward no particular destination. {full_name}: a capable administrator of a world the rest of history regarded as an open wound, who preferred not to think about that too hard. The managed Reich endured because efficient evil is harder to dislodge than passionate evil. You were good at your job.

endings.title: The Iron Reich
endings.reason: The dream turned back to iron. The interstellar fleets launched under Reich colors; the subject worlds received the same paperwork Earth's conquered zones had. {full_name} built the procurement empire that made expansion possible, his name attached to contracts for things the contracts did not name. What the Reich did to {the_nation} it now did at the scale of solar systems. History — the history that could be spoken aloud in this timeline — called it manifest destiny. The other history was written in the margins of documents classified at the highest tier, and it will not survive the archive purge.

endings.title: The Covenant Commonwealth
endings.reason: The Covenant took root in a way its founders had argued about in seminaries for decades — not as a mandate but as a covenant actually lived. The hospitals ran on tithing surplus. The courts arbitrated in verse and came out approximately just. {full_name} held the economic portfolio of a nation that genuinely believed itself chosen and had, by most measurable indicators, made something worth believing in. Whether God ordained it is a question you left to theologians. You built the fiscal architecture. The Commonwealth endures.

endings.title: The Established Church-State
endings.reason: The theocracy stabilized into something neither visionary nor oppressive — a faith-flavored bureaucracy where Sunday attendance was socially mandatory, the curriculum taught creation science alongside physics, and the courts referred disputes to scripture before precedent. {full_name} moved money through it all, neither a true believer nor a cynic, simply a man who understood that this was the system and the system paid. The established church-state endures the way all established things do: through the low-grade coercion of normalcy.

endings.title: Gilead
endings.reason: The Handmaid's dystopia that the sermons always promised was mercy became, in practice, the thing the sermons described. The dress codes came first, then the role assignments, then the re-education facilities that everyone understood were not really about re-education. {full_name} financed the infrastructure and did not ask what it was for. Power is power; {head_of_state} had it; {full_name} serviced it. The nation that emerged calls itself God's country. It is technically correct.

endings.title: Post-Scarcity Commonwealth
endings.reason: The board dissolved itself into a foundation. The automation dividend, rather than accruing to the ten thousand who owned the machines, was constitutionally mandated to flow outward — a structural victory won after decades of strategic argument and one historic vote that {full_name} cast as the swing position. {the_nation} entered an era of enforced abundance that the economists said was mathematically impossible and that happened anyway. {full_name}'s reputation, historically, is of a man who understood early what his peers learned too late: you cannot spend it faster than a revolution takes it.

endings.title: The Managed Oligopoly
endings.reason: The consolidation held at a stable plateau — monopolies capped by treaty, dividends regulated to prevent the revolution the last century had previewed, the economy humming in the comfortable medium of efficient unfreedom. {full_name} sat near the top of this arrangement and called it success. By the metrics that counted — market cap, longevity of holdings, dynasties established — it was. The system was not fair; it was stable, which is the thing systems prize above fairness every time the choice presents itself.

endings.title: The Company-Town Earth
endings.reason: The debt peonage completed its logic. The automation dividend never redistributed; the displaced workforce signed contracts of indenture without calling them that; the company towns spread until there was nothing outside them. {full_name} owned the largest nodes of this system and collected rents from people who had no alternative to paying them. What the economists called equilibrium the people living in it called a cage. Both descriptions were accurate. The cage was very well managed.

endings.title: The Charitable Ministry
endings.reason: The empire of faith turned its machinery outward. The disaster-relief network became the largest non-state logistical system in existence; the counseling infrastructure trained more therapists than the medical system; the tithing surplus funded water projects across three continents. {full_name} stood at the financial center of this and — unusually — the thing it funded was actually what it said it was funding. The brand prospered because the substance was real. The congregation grew. The books balanced. The wells in the desert worked.

endings.title: The Prosperity Empire
endings.reason: The formula never changed because it never needed to: promise heaven, charge for access to the waiting room, and reinvest the surplus in more visible promises. {full_name} ran the financial architecture of an organization that had outgrown religion and become entertainment, therapy, and social network simultaneously. The tithing was technically voluntary. The social pressure was not. The empire prospered. Whether it helped anyone is a question its constituents had long since stopped posing.

endings.title: The Cult of Personality
endings.reason: The congregation stopped being a congregation and became a movement, and the movement stopped having an ideology and became a body that pointed where its leader pointed. {given_name} {surname} at the center of this: a man whose name the faithful spoke the way earlier generations spoke the name of God, and whose financial empire was indistinguishable from his spiritual one because those had never really been separate things. Dissent was apostasy. Apostasy had consequences. The denomination grows.

endings.title: The Enlightened Broadcast
endings.reason: The platform turned — slowly, expensively, after a generation of editorial fights — toward something it had resisted for decades: accuracy. The investigative arm was funded properly; the audience metrics were decoupled from the revenue model; the stories that needed telling got the airtime they had always been crowded out of. {full_name} held the commercial structure together while the editorial side did what editorial sides do when they are actually free to do it. The ratings were not better. The republic was.

endings.title: The Spectacle Machine
endings.reason: The platform won by becoming essential and neutral, which meant it had an opinion about nothing and a reach into everything. {full_name} presided over the largest attention-harvesting apparatus in history and called it journalism, which it sometimes was. The algorithmically served outrage kept the subscriptions up; the horse-race coverage kept the politicians nervous in the productive ways; the documentary arm won prizes. The system was not propaganda. It was something subtler — a mirror aimed at the most profitable angle of the room.

endings.title: The Propaganda State
endings.reason: The distinction between the network and the government dissolved so gradually that the historians later disagreed about which year it had technically completed. {full_name}'s platforms broadcast what {head_of_state} required and suppressed what {head_of_state} forbade, and the population, marinated in it from infancy, lost the ability to ask the difference. The ratings were excellent. The staff wrote copy they believed in, which was the most unsettling thing about all of it. The propaganda state endures because it does not need to force anyone to lie.

endings.title: The Pacific Techno-Frontier Commonwealth
endings.reason: The Pacific rim built the thing the rest of the century had theorized: governance by council, infrastructure by commons, research by open license. {the_nation}'s western edge became the proving ground that the model actually worked at scale — not because of ideology but because the catastrophe-proofing math required cooperation and the cooperation, once structural, turned out to produce better outcomes than competition. {full_name} held the investment architecture and found, to his own surprise, that a commonwealth was a good place to compound returns. The frontier remained open. The commons held.

endings.title: The Managed Pacific Republic
endings.reason: The secession did not radicalize — it institutionalized. The Pacific republic ran its carbon markets, its tech-sector regulations, its ranked-choice senate, and its universal healthcare system with the competent blandness of a well-funded NGO that had somehow acquired nuclear submarines. {full_name} operated at the intersection of the investment networks and the policy apparatus, which in the Pacific republic were politely acknowledged to be the same thing. The republic was genuinely better than what it left. It was also not what anyone had dreamed.

endings.title: The Coastal Tech-Oligarch Fiefdom
endings.reason: The tech sector did not disrupt the power structure — it absorbed it. The venture capital firms held the regulatory agencies; the platform networks held the information infrastructure; the campuses held the housing. {full_name} built the financial layer beneath all of this and collected the systematic rent on everything that moved through it. The population of the coastal fiefdom had better phones than anywhere else on Earth and significantly less say in how they lived. They called it innovation. The feudal analogy was technically imprecise and functionally exact.

endings.title: The Muddled Middle
endings.reason: No utopia. No collapse. The levers moved in both directions and averaged out to the status quo, which is another name for a world that exhausted itself preventing the worst without ever assembling the will for the better. {full_name} accumulated and governed and aged in a {the_nation} that functioned at roughly the level it always had — unequal, contentious, intermittently competent, frequently embarrassing, occasionally great. History filed it under 'continued.' The dynasty endured. The country muddled through. That is, statistically, the most common outcome.
